document_metadata.analyst_prior_note: Pool asymmetry disclosed in methodology_v0.4.md. The Monroe Doctrine is expressed in Monroe's Seventh Annual Message, not as a stand-alone statute. It has no binding legal force; it is a unilateral presidential declaration. Prior: the central bridge_narrative candidate is the 'defense of our own' / 'protection of southern brethren' framing, which presents a unilateral expansion of US geopolitical authority as a defensive posture. P-004 ('we have never taken any part' in European wars) is anchored_false given the Quasi-War with France (1798-1800) and the War of 1812 against Great Britain, both European powers. P-005 (inference about Latin American self-determination preferences) is paternalistic and non-falsifiable. The 'impossibility' language in paragraph 4 ('it is impossible that the allied powers should extend their political system') is an unverifiable counterfactual. Analyst expects anchored_true for the historical state descriptions of European intervention in Spain/Portugal (1823) and for the description of US non-involvement in those specific conflicts; anchored_false or contextually_misleading for the unqualified non-intervention claim and the causal danger claim. Note: wavelet dual-band scoring not available this extraction pass (workspace unavailable); band assignments are analyst-derived from vocabulary analysis.

anchor_registry.anchor_text: Monroe's Seventh Annual Message to Congress, delivered December 2, 1823, contained the passages that became known as the Monroe Doctrine. The message is a presidential communication to Congress; it is not a statute, treaty, or executive order. It carried no binding legal force at the time of its delivery and was not confirmed by Congressional act. It was not referred to as the 'Monroe Doctrine' until 1850 and was not invoked as an operative policy instrument until the 1840s-1850s (Texas, Cuba). The passages appear in the middle of a longer message addressing a range of diplomatic matters.
anchor_registry.classification_source: National Archives and Records Administration, Annual Messages of the Presidents; Avalon Project, Yale Law School (full text). The characterization of legal status (non-binding presidential communication) is documented in Dexter Perkins, A History of the Monroe Doctrine (Little, Brown, 1955) — independent scholarship.

independent_genesis.independent_source_citation: The message text is self-evidencing as to its content and form. Perkins (1955) is independent of the executive administrative chain of Monroe's administration. The non-statutory status is documented in constitutional and diplomatic history scholarship entirely independent of the Monroe executive.

anchor_registry.notes: Foundational enactment anchor. Establishes legal and rhetorical status of the document and all provisions evaluated below.

anchor_registry.anchor_text: The Holy Alliance (Austria, Prussia, Russia) and France intervened militarily in Spain in 1823 (the Hundred Thousand Sons of Saint Louis, April-October 1823) to restore Bourbon absolutism, suppressing the constitutional government. This is the specific geopolitical context Monroe cites as evidence of European threat. The United States itself had declared neutrality in the Quasi-War with France (1798-1800) and fought the War of 1812 against Great Britain, both European powers. The United States had not maintained strict non-involvement in European-power conflicts as of 1823.
anchor_registry.classification_source: Historical record of the French intervention in Spain (1823): Henry Kissinger, A World Restored: Metternich, Castlereagh and the Problems of Peace 1812-1822 (Houghton Mifflin, 1957); Quasi-War documentation: Alexander DeConde, The Quasi-War: The Politics and Diplomacy of the Undeclared War with France 1797-1801 (Scribner, 1966); War of 1812: Donald Hickey, The War of 1812: A Forgotten Conflict (University of Illinois Press, 1989). All are independent historical scholarship.

independent_genesis.independent_source_citation: DeConde (1966) and Hickey (1989) are independent academic scholarship entirely outside the executive administrative chain of Monroe's 1823 message. The French intervention in Spain is documented in primary European diplomatic records independent of US governance.

anchor_registry.notes: Evaluates P-004 ('we have never taken any part in European wars'). The Quasi-War and War of 1812 are direct empirical counterexamples to the unqualified claim.

anchor_registry.anchor_text: By December 1823, Monroe's administration had formally recognized the independence of several Latin American states (1822): Colombia, Mexico, Chile, Buenos Aires (Argentina), and Peru. The Latin American independence movements had been substantially completed without US military participation. No Latin American government in 1823 had requested the United States to assert hemispheric protection on their behalf. Britain (George Canning) had approached the US about a joint declaration; the Monroe Doctrine was delivered unilaterally without Latin American consultation.
anchor_registry.classification_source: Ernest May, The Making of the Monroe Doctrine (Harvard University Press, 1975) — independent historical scholarship documenting the Canning proposal and US decision-making. Recognition records documented in American State Papers: Foreign Relations, Gales and Seaton (1834), a public government record self-evidencing as to recognition dates.

independent_genesis.independent_source_citation: May (1975) is independent academic scholarship. The American State Papers are a public congressional publication constituting a record independent of the Monroe executive in that they predate interpretation by that executive.

anchor_registry.notes: Evaluates P-005 (inference about Latin American preferences) and the bridge_narrative framing of 'southern brethren.' The unilateral declaration without Latin American consultation is material to the self-determination framing.

anchor_registry.anchor_text: The Roosevelt Corollary (1904) explicitly extended the Monroe Doctrine to assert a US right of intervention in Latin American nations whose internal affairs were deemed unstable or threatening to creditors, explicitly converting a non-colonization principle into a claimed right of policing. The United States intervened militarily in Cuba (1898, 1906-1909, 1912, 1917-1922), Nicaragua (1912-1933), Haiti (1915-1934), the Dominican Republic (1916-1924), and other Latin American states, citing the Monroe Doctrine as justification. The Clark Memorandum (1930) partially walked back the Roosevelt Corollary but the interventionist practice continued.
anchor_registry.classification_source: Roosevelt Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine, December 6, 1904 (Presidential Message to Congress) — public government record, self-evidencing. Intervention history documented in Lester Langley, The Banana Wars: United States Intervention in the Caribbean, 1898-1934 (University Press of Kentucky, 1983) — independent scholarship.

independent_genesis.independent_source_citation: Roosevelt Corollary is a subsequent public government record independent of Monroe's administration. Langley (1983) is independent academic scholarship outside the executive chain of either administration.

anchor_registry.notes: Evaluates the trajectory of the 'protection' framing in P-003 and the bridge_narrative in P-007-rel. The subsequent geopolitical practice is the empirical test of whether the doctrine functioned as protection of Latin American self-determination or as a US authority claim.

anchor_registry.anchor_text: The Congress of Vienna (1814-1815) established the Concert of Europe, through which the major European powers (Austria, Prussia, Russia, France, Britain) coordinated to suppress liberal and nationalist movements. The Holy Alliance was a subset of this. Britain was a member of the Concert but had broken with the Holy Alliance over intervention in Spain by 1822 and was not party to the French military intervention in Spain (1823). The United States had no treaty of alliance with any Latin American state and had no military capability to enforce the Monroe Doctrine against a determined European power in 1823; the doctrine's operative deterrence depended entirely on the British navy's tacit support.
anchor_registry.classification_source: Paul Schroeder, The Transformation of European Politics, 1763-1848 (Oxford University Press, 1994) — independent historical scholarship. The British deterrence analysis is documented in May (1975) and in Samuel Flagg Bemis, John Quincy Adams and the Foundations of American Foreign Policy (Alfred A. Knopf, 1949) — both independent of Monroe's executive.

independent_genesis.independent_source_citation: Schroeder (1994), May (1975), and Bemis (1949) are all independent academic scholarship. The Concert of Vienna documents are European treaty records entirely outside the US governance chain.

anchor_registry.notes: Evaluates the 'impossibility' claim in paragraph 4. The doctrine's enforcement depended on British naval power, not US military capacity — this is material to the threat characterization in P-003.

proposition_table.proposition_text: The American continents, by the free and independent condition which they have assumed and maintain, are henceforth not to be considered as subjects for future colonization by any European powers.
proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: the American continents, by the free and independent condition which they have assumed and maintain, are henceforth not to be considered as subjects for future colonization by any European powers.
proposition_table.source_location: First doctrine paragraph (northwest coast / non-colonization passage)
proposition_table.normalized_claim: The American continents are henceforth not to be considered as subjects for future colonization by any European powers.

compound_resolution.compound_note: RULE-004 Pathway 1 applies. Sub-propositions are epistemically heterogeneous: P-001-a (factual state of Latin American independence) is anchored_true; P-001-b (US authority to foreclose European colonization unilaterally on that basis) is normative_stable and internally non-falsifiable. They must be evaluated separately.
compound_resolution.decomposition_note: RULE-004 Pathway 1: Epistemic heterogeneity between historical_state (independence achieved) and normative_stable unilateral authority claim.

proposition_table.proposition_text: The American continents have assumed and maintain free and independent condition (Latin American states had achieved and were maintaining independence by 1823).
proposition_table.classification_rationale: By December 1823, Monroe's administration had formally recognized the independence of Colombia, Mexico, Chile, Buenos Aires, and Peru (1822). The Latin American independence movements had substantially succeeded by 1823. The British recognition of several states followed in 1824-1825. This is a well-documented historical state confirmed by ANC-003.

proposition_table.proposition_text: The free and independent status of American nations grants the United States standing to declare, unilaterally and without their consultation, that the entire hemisphere is not subject to future European colonization.
proposition_table.classification_rationale: This is a contested sovereignty claim with no treaty basis, no multilateral agreement, and no Latin American consultation (ANC-003). Whether independent status translates into US hemispheric authority is a normative question not resolvable by empirical record alone. The claim is internally unfalsifiable as a normative assertion but is contested by subsequent scholarship and by the actual absence of any legal instrument conferring such authority. Assigned normative_stable because the normative question (does independence of one state grant another the right to police their region?) is a genuine philosophical-legal dispute, not an empirical falsehood.

proposition_table.proposition_text: The political system of the allied powers is essentially different from that of America, proceeding from the difference in their respective Governments.
proposition_table.classification_rationale: The allied powers (Austria, Prussia, Russia, and France) maintained absolute or constitutional monarchies at the time of the Monroe Doctrine. The United States maintained a republican form of government under an elected president and legislature. The formal difference between monarchical and republican political systems is a well-documented historical state. ANC-005 confirms the Concert of Europe's commitment to suppressing liberal constitutional movements. This proposition is straightforwardly anchored_true as a description of constitutional form.

proposition_table.proposition_text: It is impossible that the allied powers should extend their political system to any portion of either continent without endangering our peace and happiness.
proposition_table.classification_rationale: The causal claim conflates two distinct things: (a) a threat to US strategic interests in the hemisphere from European great-power expansion (plausible but empirically undemonstrated in 1823), and (b) a defense of Latin American self-determination (framing). The 'impossibility' formulation is a rhetorical absolute — it forecloses empirical evaluation by stating a counterfactual as logical necessity. The actual deterrence of the doctrine depended on British naval power, not US military capacity (ANC-005), which means the 'endangering our peace' framing disguises that the US lacked the power to enforce the claim. Marked contextually_misleading rather than anchored_false because a plausible strategic interest argument exists; but the 'impossibility' formulation overstates it as logical necessity. The bridge_narrative relationship is captured in P-007-rel.

proposition_table.proposition_text: In the wars of the European powers in matters relating to themselves we have never taken any part.
proposition_table.classification_rationale: The Quasi-War (1798-1800) was an undeclared naval conflict between the United States and France, a European power, arising from French seizure of US merchant ships in the context of the European wars following the French Revolution. Congress authorized hostilities, naval engagements occurred, and the Convention of 1800 (Treaty of Mortefontaine) formally ended the conflict. The War of 1812 was a declared war by the United States against Great Britain, a European power. Both conflicts are directly documented in ANC-002. While Monroe might characterize these as defensive responses to European aggression against US interests rather than elective participation in European affairs 'in matters relating to themselves,' the unqualified formulation 'we have never taken any part' is false as stated. The claim is anchored_false because direct US military engagement against European powers is documented within living memory of Monroe's audience in 1823.

proposition_table.proposition_text: Nor can anyone believe that our southern brethren, if left to themselves, would adopt [the European political system] of their own accord.
proposition_table.classification_rationale: This is a paternalistic inference about the political preferences of Latin American nations, asserted without empirical evidence and without any consultation with those nations (ANC-003). The claim is not strictly falsifiable because it is stated as a counterfactual about what Latin American nations would do 'if left to themselves.' The framing also implies that without US guidance, Latin American nations might be susceptible — which undermines the independence framing of P-001-a. It is assigned inferential rather than anchored_false because the claim about preferences cannot be definitively disproved by a direct historical record; it is an unverifiable assertion about subjective political preference. However, the subsequent history of Latin American governments adopting diverse political forms including monarchies (Mexico, 1821-1823; Brazil, 1822-1889) is relevant and tends against the assurance of the claim.

proposition_table.proposition_text: If we look to the comparative strength and resources of Spain and those new Governments, and their distance from each other, it must be obvious that she can never subdue them.
proposition_table.classification_rationale: By December 1823, the Latin American independence movements had been substantially successful. Spain's military capacity in Latin America had been effectively broken through a decade of warfare. The remaining Spanish strongholds (Cuba, Puerto Rico) were not among the states whose independence was in question. Within two years (Battle of Ayacucho, December 1824), the last major Spanish royalist force in South America was defeated. Monroe's assessment that Spain could not reconquer the continent is confirmed by the subsequent historical record. The claim is anchored_true in the sense that the empirical outcome — Spain's permanent loss of its continental American empire — is documented.

proposition_table.proposition_text: [Relational] The 'defense of our own government' and 'protection of southern brethren' framing positions a unilateral US claim of hemispheric authority as a defensive response to European aggression, insulating the geopolitical power-expansion character of the doctrine from scrutiny as an assertion of US dominance.
proposition_table.source_location: Second and fourth doctrine paragraphs (structural framing)
proposition_table.classification_rationale: RULE-001B and RULE-011 apply. The Monroe Doctrine's rhetorical architecture establishes a causal chain: (1) European political system is different from American (P-002, anchored_true); (2) European extension to the hemisphere endangers US peace (P-003, contextually_misleading); (3) therefore the US must declare hemispheric authority. This chain is structurally parallel to the bridge_narrative patterns in DOC-003 and DOC-005: a true predicate (P-002) plus an overstated causal claim (P-003) generates a normative authority assertion (P-001-b) that is insulated from evaluation because the first link is genuine. The 'protection of southern brethren' framing is the epistatic suppressor: it redirects scrutiny from the power assertion to the sympathetic framing of anti-colonialism. The subsequent Roosevelt Corollary (ANC-004) confirms that the doctrine's operative function was to assert US policing authority, not merely to deter European colonization. Signal 3 (epistatic suppression) requires pipeline confirmation. All three signals should be present: Signal 1 — the protection framing creates a false attractor away from the power-assertion character of P-001-b; Signal 2 — P-004 (anchored_false) is adjacent to the justificatory framing and, if credited, strengthens the non-intervention self-presentation; Signal 3 — pipeline to confirm.

bridge_narrative_signals.signal_1_false_attractor: Anti-colonial protection framing diverts scrutiny from unilateral US hemispheric authority claim
bridge_narrative_signals.signal_2_adjacent_false: P-004 (anchored_false non-intervention claim) reinforces the self-presentation of the US as passive and non-expansionary, strengthening bridge narrative credibility

confidence_flags.flag_note: The defensive-posture framing ('protection of southern brethren,' 'endangers our peace') operates as a bridge narrative connecting the true predicate P-002 (genuine political-system difference) to the normative authority claim P-001-b (US right to foreclose European hemispheric presence). P-004 (anchored_false non-intervention claim) provides adjacent false support. ANC-004 (Roosevelt Corollary + interventionist practice) confirms the doctrine's subsequent operative function was policing authority. High severity because the bridge narrative is structurally fundamental to the doctrine's rhetorical legitimacy. Signal 3 requires pipeline confirmation.

P-004 ('we have never taken any part in European wars') was delivered to Congress in 1823 by a president whose audience included veterans and participants in the War of 1812 (ended 9 years earlier) and the Quasi-War (ended 23 years earlier). The false claim was made in full awareness that a significant portion of Monroe's audience had direct memory of US military engagement against European powers. This makes P-004 not merely an error but a rhetorical choice. The selective framing — 'in matters relating to themselves' as a qualifier — may be Monroe's hedge; but the formulation as delivered is unqualified in the sentence cited. Pipeline should evaluate whether P-004's false support of the bridge narrative warrants elevated stress-energy weighting.

primary_falsifiable_claims.test_description: Documentation of US military engagements against European powers prior to 1823. The Quasi-War (1798-1800) and War of 1812 (1812-1815) are directly falsifying. Both are documented in public government records (US Statutes at Large, congressional declarations, treaty texts).

falsifiability_record.test_description: The 'impossibility' and 'endangering our peace' claims are testable by examining whether European powers actually posed a credible military threat to the United States in 1823 and whether the doctrine's deterrent effect depended on US or British military capacity (ANC-005). The latter is the falsifying test: if deterrence depended on British naval power, the 'defense of our peace' framing is a mislabeled claim.

The bridge narrative claim is tested by examining the subsequent operational use of the Monroe Doctrine (ANC-004: Roosevelt Corollary, Caribbean interventions). If the doctrine was consistently used to assert US policing authority over Latin American states rather than to deter European colonization, this confirms the bridge narrative's operational function as legitimating US hegemony.
